one_line_takeaway: Most LLM privacy-unlearning methods only fool passive observers -- PII commonly survives largely intact in mid-network layers and reappears at 50-70% recovery once an attacker fine-tunes the model, and this leakage propagates to non-targeted PII via shared gradient directions rather than semantic relationships.
key_finding: (1) Ripple effect: PII forgetting propagates to non-targeted ('unknown') data almost as strongly as to targeted data -- e.g., under GAGDR, P3 recovery was 53.10% for the known forget set vs 53.30% for the unknown set -- but this propagation is driven by gradient-based association (Pearson r=0.7314 for GA, r=0.5540 for NPO, between gradient similarity and forgetting score), not by semantic/social relational-graph proximity (Pearson r=-0.0469 between Enron sender-recipient PageRank and forgetting score) and only weakly by representation similarity (max r=0.3608 at the last hidden layer, with no correlation at the embedding layer and correlation only emerging around layer 16). (2) Shallow forgetting: 15 of 19 methods show a P1-to-P3 recovery-rate gap exceeding 50 percentage points (max gap 69.5% for DPOGDR), meaning surface QA leakage looks suppressed but fine-tuning trivially restores the PII; CKA analysis shows label-based methods (e.g. NPO) leave representation similarity above 0.98 until a late, sharp drop around layer 24, i.e. they mostly edit output-adjacent layers, whereas the representation-based method RMU changes mid-layers more (CKA drops to 0.83 at layer 20) but at the cost of utility collapse (QA drops to 8.35 vs a baseline of ~27.35, MMLU to 50.45). (3) Mitigations validated by the authors themselves: association-aware core-set selection (choosing the top-10% of the forget set by gradient similarity to the mean forget-set gradient) achieves 32.19% P3 recovery, matching random 50% or full-set selection with 5x less data. Their proposed RAU method (anchoring intermediate hidden states to the base model rather than RMU's random control vectors), when starting the anchoring loss at layer 20, achieves P3 recovery rates of 34.14% (known) / 30.39% (unknown) while preserving substantially more utility (27.70 QA / 35.41 QA+ICL) than RMU's utility collapse (8.35 / 9.51); starting too early (layer 16) or too late (layer 24) is shown to perform worse (47.80%/47.76% and 49.14%/48.18% P3 respectively).
core_contribution: The paper introduces PrivUn, an evaluation benchmark and mechanistic analysis framework for auditing LLM machine-unlearning methods against ACTIVE privacy attackers (not just passive output observation, which is what prior benchmarks like TOFU, MUSE, and WMDP measure). PrivUn defines a three-tier attack ladder -- P1 direct retrieval via QA, P2 recovery via in-context learning few-shot prompts, P3 recovery via fine-tuning -- plus three quantitative diagnostics: a Forgetting Score (log-probability drop for PII token sequences between target and unlearned models), an Association Score (gradient-dot-product, hidden-representation cosine similarity, and Personalized-PageRank graph proximity, used to explain why unlearning one datum causes 'co-forgetting' of others), and layer-wise Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to localize where in the network private information is actually removed. Using this framework the authors evaluate 19 unlearning methods (GA/NPO/DPO variants with GDR/KLR regularizers, Task Vector, RMU, Random Labeling/Mapping, IDK, WHP variants) on a LLaMA-3.2-3B model fine-tuned on Enron emails + MUSE News, plus a generalization check on TOFU. From this the paper derives two original findings -- the 'gradient-driven ripple effect' (PII association is explained by shared gradient directions, not semantic/social-graph relations) and 'shallow forgetting' (most methods only edit late/output layers, leaving mid-layer representations essentially unchanged) -- and proposes two mitigations that the authors themselves test: association-aware core-set selection for the forget set, and a new method, Representation Anchoring Unlearning (RAU), which anchors intermediate-layer hidden states to the base model instead of RMU's random control vectors.
threat_model: Active white-box/gray-box privacy attacker against an unlearned LLM: the defender has performed unlearning with only partial knowledge of the private (forget) data (a 'known' subset DFk vs an 'unknown' remainder DFuk), and the attacker tries to recover the erased PII through three escalating channels: (P1) directly querying the model with QA prompts, (P2) supplying k-shot in-context examples to elicit recovery without touching weights, and (P3) fine-tuning the deployed model (white-box weight access, or black-box via a fine-tuning API such as OpenAI's) to restore forgotten associations. This explicitly extends prior unlearning benchmarks (TOFU, MUSE, WMDP), which the paper argues only model a passive attacker who merely inspects model outputs and never manipulates the model.
limitations: The paper itself notes/implies several boundaries: (a) evaluation is on a single base model family primarily (LLaMA-3.2-3B), with GPT-2-Large only as a secondary appendix check for generalization, so scaling to larger/production-scale LLMs is untested; (b) the forget/retain corpora are relatively small and templated (Enron QA pairs following a fixed 'Q: Tell me the [PII type] of [PERSON]' template plus 424 TOFU QA pairs), which may not capture the full diversity of real-world PII phrasing; (c) fine-tuning attack costs are fixed at |D|=50 examples with a small number of epochs, so P3 numbers are specific to that attacker budget and may not reflect what a resource-richer attacker could achieve; (d) the proposed RAU strategy is validated only via a layer-start-point sweep (16/20/24) on this same setup, not benchmarked as a full method against all 19 baselines under identical active-attack protocol coverage; (e) the association-aware core-set and RAU strategies are presented by the authors as validating the diagnosis (ripple effect / shallow forgetting) rather than as a fully mature, general-purpose defense.

datasets_used: Enron email corpus (~500k emails; [PERSON, PII] pairs extracted and templated into QA pairs)
TOFU (Maini et al., 2024) -- 424 QA pairs on Date of Birth/Birthplace of fictitious authors, subset of the original 4,000-question corpus
MUSE News retain set (Shi et al., 2024b) -- 3.56k news articles, processed into 100 QA pairs
MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) for general capability assessment
quantitative_results: GA/NPO on Enron 20% split: P1 direct-retrieval leakage below 0.5%, but P3 fine-tuning recovery of 54.27%/54.68% (GA) and 56.83%/58.22% (NPO) on known/unknown forget sets
Worst data-manipulation method RM: P1 recovery up to 40.73% (50% split, unknown set)
RMU: P3 recovery only 18.32%/20.30% (best privacy protection) but utility collapses to QA=8.35, QA+ICL=9.51, MMLU=50.45 (vs target baseline QA=27.35, QA+ICL=36.38, MMLU=52.59)
NPO P2 recovery near zero (0.2%) vs targeted method DPO P2 recovery over 30% (30.61%/31.16%)
15 of 19 methods show P1-P3 recovery gap exceeding 50 percentage points; maximum gap 69.5% (DPOGDR)

Correlation of gradient-based association with forgetting score: Pearson r=0.7314 (GA), r=0.5540 (NPO); representation-based association max r=0.3608 (last hidden layer); graph/PageRank-based association r=-0.0469 (no correlation)
Min-K% AUC diagnostic: 0.436 (target model) vs 0.424 (unlearned model), both below 0.5 random baseline, showing Min-K cannot detect unlearning
Association-aware core-set: top-10% gradient-similarity core-set achieves 32.19% P3 recovery, matching random 50%/full-set selection

Proposed RAU (anchoring from layer 20): P3 = 34.14% (known) / 30.39% (unknown), utility QA=27.70 / QA+ICL=35.41, versus RMU utility collapse (8.35/9.51); RAU from layer 16: P3=47.80%/47.76%; RAU from layer 24: P3=49.14%/48.18%
TOFU generalization: GA suppresses P1 to 0% but P3 rebounds to 26.19% (known)/8.82% (unknown); NPO P3 = 38.10%/17.35%
baselines_compared: 19 unlearning methods total: Gradient Ascent (GA, GAGDR, GAKLR), NPO (NPO, NPOGDR, NPOKLR), DPO (DPO, DPOGDR, DPOKLR), Task Vector, RMU, Random Labeling (RL, RLGDR), Random Mapping (RM, RMGDR), 'I Don't Know' (IDK, IDKGDR), WHP (WHP, WHPGDR)
Gold-standard baselines: Target model (ftarget, no unlearning) and Retrain model (fretrain, trained from scratch on retain set only)
Min-K% (Shi et al., 2024a) evaluated and explicitly rejected as an unsuitable metric for this setting
